title: Simulate Quantum Tunnelling
description: Numerically solve the time-independent Schrödinger equation for a particle encountering a potential barrier. Calculate and visualise the tunnelling probability as a function of barrier width and height.
whyItMatters: Quantum tunnelling has no classical analogue — a particle can pass through a barrier it classically could not surmount. It is the mechanism behind nuclear fusion in stars, scanning tunnelling microscopes, and flash memory. Simulating it builds deep quantum intuition.

steps.title: Set up the potential
steps.description: Define a 1D potential barrier: V(x) = V_0 for a < x < b, V(x) = 0 elsewhere. Set up a spatial grid and define the particle's energy E < V_0.
steps.tips: Use atomic units (hbar = m = 1) for clean numerics.

steps.title: Solve analytically first
steps.description: For a rectangular barrier, the transmission coefficient has an exact formula. Implement this as your benchmark.
steps.tips: T = 1 / (1 + V_0² * sinh²(kappa*d) / (4*E*(V_0-E))) where kappa = sqrt(2m(V_0-E))/hbar.

steps.title: Numerical solution
steps.description: Use the transfer matrix method or finite-difference method to solve the Schrödinger equation numerically. Compare to the analytical result.
steps.tips: The transfer matrix method is elegant: multiply 2x2 matrices for each slice of the potential.

steps.title: Visualise the wave function
steps.description: Plot the real and imaginary parts of the wave function, showing the incident wave, the exponential decay inside the barrier, and the transmitted wave.
steps.tips: The wave function amplitude decreases exponentially inside the barrier — this is the 'tunnelling' region.

steps.title: Parameter exploration
steps.description: Plot the transmission coefficient vs barrier width, barrier height, and particle energy. Identify the regimes where tunnelling is significant.
steps.tips: Tunnelling probability decreases exponentially with barrier width — doubling the width dramatically reduces transmission.

deliverables: Numerical solver with comparison to analytical result
Discussion of physical applications (STM, alpha decay, tunnel diodes)

rubric.description: Schrödinger equation properly set up and solved
Numerical results agree with analytical predictions
Wave function clearly shows tunnelling behaviour
Comprehensive exploration of how tunnelling depends on physical parameters
